rationale: This is exceptional profile writing. Every sentence earns its place. The opening sequence — arriving in the dark, thermos, transistor radio, the rejected earbuds — builds character through precise, concrete detail rather than adjectives. The structural move from physical labor ('the real work of his morning is already finished') to the emotional labor of greeting students is elegant and perfectly timed. The specificity is outstanding: 'the girl with the purple hearing aids,' 'the boy who eats lunch alone near the vending machines,' the thermos of black coffee, the loading dock entrance near the gymnasium. The dialogue snippets feel natural and varied, each one revealing a different kind of relationship. The language is plain and direct throughout — no inflated phrasing, no throat-clearing. The voice is warm and human without being sentimental. The piece is cut off, which is its only flaw, but what exists on the page is essentially flawless magazine-quality profile writing.
